parsed.summary: Cannot complete a meaningful adversarial review: the repository context provides only file names and diff stats, not the actual code content. The diff spans 84 files and ~6,600 lines across core runtime modules (retry, error dispatch, API client, state, job tracking, filesystem), CI, and hooks — a large blast radius for a patch-level bump. Several structural red flags are visible from the stat alone, but concrete findings require reading the actual source. Shipping without line-level review of the new retry/error-handling subsystem and the deleted process.mjs is not justified.

findings.title: Deletion of scripts/lib/process.mjs may leave dangling imports
findings.body: The diff stat shows scripts/lib/process.mjs with 67 lines removed and 0 added — the entire file was deleted. If any remaining module (glm-client, glm-companion, render, etc.) still contains `import ... from './process.mjs'` or `from './lib/process.mjs'`, Node will throw ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND at runtime. The concurrent modification to scripts/check-imports.mjs (5 lines changed) suggests import paths were updated, but without reading the actual code we cannot confirm all call sites were migrated. A missed import would only surface in production under the specific code path that exercises it.
findings.recommendation: Run `grep -r 'process\.mjs' scripts/` against the current tree to confirm zero references remain. Add a CI gate that fails on unresolved imports.

findings.title: New retry + BigModel error-dispatch subsystem is high-risk and unreviewed
findings.body: retry.mjs (203 new lines) and bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 new lines) form a new error-classification and retry/backoff subsystem integrated into glm-client.mjs (155 lines changed). Retry logic is a classic source of: (a) infinite loops when error classification returns 'retryable' for non-transient failures, (b) thundering-herd amplification when jitter is missing from exponential backoff, (c) silent success masking when retries discard the original error context, (d) timeout blowout when max-retry delays compound beyond caller expectations. The commit message says 'BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff' but without reading the actual retry bounds, jitter implementation, error-classification completeness, and integration points, we cannot confirm any of these failure modes are handled. tests/retry.test.mjs (352 lines) and tests/bigmodel-errors.test.mjs (189 lines) exist but their coverage is unknown.
findings.recommendation: Before ship: (1) read retry.mjs for max-retry ceiling, per-attempt timeout, jitter, and abort-signal propagation; (2) read bigmodel-errors.mjs for completeness of non-retryable error classification; (3) confirm tests cover retry exhaustion, classification of unknown error shapes, and concurrent retry interleaving.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs changes may silently swallow API errors behind retry layer
findings.body: glm-client.mjs was substantially modified (155 lines changed) to integrate the new retry and error-dispatch modules. The happy path is likely tested, but the concern is whether caller-facing error semantics are preserved: do callers still receive distinguishable error types (auth failure vs rate-limit vs network timeout), or does the retry layer flatten them into a generic rejection? If downstream logic (job-control, tracked-jobs, render) branches on specific error codes or status values, a change in error shape would cause silent misbehavior — e.g., a permanent 401 being retried N times then reported as a generic 'request failed' instead of 'authentication error'.
findings.recommendation: Verify that retry.mjs preserves the original error's type/code/status properties through exhaustion, and that at least one test in retry.test.mjs asserts error shape fidelity after max-retries.

findings.title: State and job-tracking changes risk orphaned or duplicate jobs under concurrency
findings.body: state.mjs (32 lines changed), tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed), and job-control.mjs (20 lines changed) were all modified in the same release. These modules collectively manage job lifecycle state. If the state file format, locking strategy, or cleanup logic changed without migration, concurrent invocations could read stale state, create duplicate job entries, or fail to clean up completed jobs. No migration script is visible in the diff. The lack of a schema-version field in state files would make format drift undetectable until a runtime parse failure.
findings.recommendation: Inspect state.mjs for a schema-version field and atomic write pattern. Confirm tracked-jobs.mjs handles concurrent writes (e.g., via file locking or atomic rename). Add a migration path if the state file format changed.

findings.title: New configure-gitea-protection.sh executes remote API calls with potential privilege escalation
findings.body: A new 126-line shell script configures Gitea branch protection via API calls. If this script is invoked during setup or CI, and the Gitea token has broader permissions than intended, it could silently modify protection rules on other branches or repos. The script name suggests it was added for internal use but it ships in the release tree.
findings.recommendation: Verify the script scopes API calls to the intended repo/branch, validates the token's permission boundary, and is not invoked automatically by any user-facing command. Consider excluding it from the published package if it is internal-only.

next_steps: Run `git diff v0.4.0..HEAD -- scripts/lib/retry.mjs scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs` and read the actual code before approving.
Run `grep -rn 'process\.mjs' scripts/` to confirm no dangling imports from the deleted module.
Run the existing test suite (`npm test` or equivalent) and confirm all 13+ test files pass on a clean checkout.
Verify retry.mjs has a hard max-retry ceiling and jitter on backoff; verify bigmodel-errors.mjs classifies 4xx auth/permission errors as non-retryable.
Inspect state.mjs for atomic writes and schema versioning; confirm no state file format change without migration.
